text.title: MoSCoW Category Definitions and Rules
text.summary: Four category definitions, test questions for Must Have, effort proportion targets, and the post-lock change escalation rule.

section.title: Must Have

rule.statement: Apply the must-test literally: a requirement is Must only if at least one is true — product fails completely without it, there is no reasonable workaround, or it is legally/contractually required.
rule.rationale: Must inflation is the #1 MoSCoW failure mode. If all three test conditions are false, the requirement is at most Should. Strict application forces real trade-off conversations.

section.p: Target: 60% of sprint/release capacity. Examples: user authentication for a SaaS, payment processing for e-commerce, core value proposition feature.

section.title: Should Have

rule.statement: Every Should Have item must have a documented workaround. If no workaround exists, re-evaluate for Must.
rule.rationale: Should without a workaround is just Must without the courage to say so. The workaround field forces honesty about what users can actually do without the feature.

section.p: Target: 20% of capacity. Examples: password reset (support can issue manual reset), search/filter (users can scroll), notifications (users can check manually).

section.title: Could Have
section.p: Nice to have. Delight features, minor enhancements, secondary integrations. Target: up to 20% of capacity; first to cut when budget tightens. Examples: dark mode, keyboard shortcuts, cosmetic improvements.

section.title: Won't Have

rule.statement: Won't Have must contain at least 3 explicitly documented exclusions. It is required, not optional.
rule.rationale: Won't Have is the most-skipped category and the most valuable. Explicit exclusions prevent scope creep mid-sprint, set stakeholder expectations, and focus discussions on what was decided.

section.p: Won't Have is "explicitly not in this release" — not "rejected forever". Examples: mobile app (web first), team features (solo focus), advanced analytics (basic first).

section.title: Effort Proportions and Lock Rule

rule.statement: Must + Should must not exceed 80% of capacity. If over budget: move Should to Could, move Could to Won't. Never move Must to Should to resolve a budget overrun.
rule.rationale: Moving Musts down to resolve budget is self-deception — it either ships broken or the Must gets added back mid-sprint, causing scope chaos.

rule.statement: Once stakeholders align and lock the matrix, changing a Must requires explicit human escalation — not a quiet edit.
rule.rationale: Post-lock Must changes reset scope expectations for everyone. Silent edits cause sprint replanning without the team knowing why scope changed.

antipattern.description: Running MoSCoW without a fixed timebox and capacity number.
antipattern.reason: Without constraints, MoSCoW degrades into a wish-list exercise. The technique requires "does this fit in X days?" to produce real trade-offs.

section.reference: Full release matrix: context, Must/Should/Could/Won't tables with effort, summary totals.
